ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Pork sausages) used as closest defensible match; no Taiwan-specific sausage code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Taiwanese sausage; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical Taiwanese sausage link weighs approximately 55 g and is commonly served grilled on a skewer as a street food snack or alongside sticky rice.

nutrient_source.title: Taiwanese Sausage (香腸) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F06110009 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this source. Saturated fat value of 9.8 g/100g is derived from the HPB record as referenced in the input health_suitability notes; monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, cholesterol, and micronutrients beyond calcium and iron are not reported in the HPB entry and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Taiwanese sausage. GI estimated at 28 (low) based on the analogous food 'sausage' (GI ~28) reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × carbohydrate_g per serving / 100 = 28 × 7.4 / 100 ≈ 2.1 (serving = 55 g; carbohydrate per serving = 13.5 × 0.55 = 7.4 g). Low GI is consistent with the high fat and protein content of this processed pork product, which slows gastric emptying.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 624 mg per 100 g — exceeds the high-sodium threshold of 600 mg/100 g; a single 55 g link delivers approximately 343 mg sodium.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Taiwanese sausage; a single link provides over 14% of the 2,300 mg daily sodium limit recommended by the AHA, and the processed-meat classification independently elevates cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar (6.7 g/100 g), high saturated fat (9.8 g/100 g), and processed-meat classification are independently associated with increased Type 2 diabetes risk per ADA guidelines.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 diabetes should limit or avoid Taiwanese sausage because its added sugar raises postprandial glucose, its saturated fat worsens insulin resistance, and regular processed-meat consumption is associated with elevated T2D incidence per the American Diabetes Association 2024 Standards of Care.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 9.8 g/100 g, sodium 624 mg/100 g, and ultra-processed cured-meat classification all elevate LDL cholesterol and blood pressure.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should avoid Taiwanese sausage; its saturated fat content exceeds 50% of the AHA daily limit per 100 g, its sodium load is high, and processed red meat is classified as a cardiovascular risk food by the AHA and WHO.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 9.8 g/100 g (exceeds 5 g threshold), sodium 624 mg/100 g worsening joint oedema, and arachidonic acid from pork amplifying inflammatory cytokines.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Taiwanese sausage; its saturated fat and arachidonic acid content promote systemic inflammation, and its high sodium load worsens RA-related joint swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Taiwanese sausage (台灣香腸, tái wān xiāng cháng) is a sweet-savory cured pork sausage originating from Taiwan and widely popular across Chinese-speaking communities in Southeast Asia, including Singapore. The sausage is made from coarsely ground pork seasoned with sugar, soy sauce, rice wine (mijiu), salt, and five-spice or white pepper, then stuffed into natural casings and air-dried or smoked before being grilled or pan-fried to order. The result is a plump, glossy sausage with a slightly caramelized exterior, a juicy and tender interior, and a distinctively sweet-savory flavor profile that distinguishes it from Western-style pork sausages. Per 100 g, it provides 360 kcal, 15.6 g protein, 27.1 g fat (including approximately 9.8 g saturated fat), and 13.5 g carbohydrate with 6.7 g sugar, reflecting the added sugar in the recipe. Sodium is high at 624 mg/100 g. Clinically, Taiwanese sausage is a high-concern food for hypertension (high sodium), Type 2 diabetes (added sugar and saturated fat worsening insulin resistance), and cardiovascular disease (saturated fat and processed-meat classification). It is commonly served as street food on a skewer, often paired with sticky rice (糯米飯), garlic slices, and pickled vegetables. It is not suitable for halal or vegetarian diets.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Taiwanese sausage?
Is Taiwanese sausage high in sodium?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F06110009, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). The HPB entry is an approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. Saturated fat is referenced from the same HPB record as cited in the input health_suitability notes. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (sausage category, GI ~28), which is the approved GI source.
notes: 1. dietary_fibre_g is set to -1: the HPB Foodo entry for this record reports dietary fibre as not available (sentinel value -1.0 in input). Dietary fibre data for this specific Taiwanese sausage preparation is not reported in the HPB database as of 2026-04-16. USDA FoodData Central entry for pork sausage (NDB 10165) reports 0 g dietary fibre, but this value has not been adopted here as the HPB record is the primary source and the formulation differs. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, and all vitamin and mineral fields beyond calcium_mg and iron_mg are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, cholesterol, and micronutrient data for this specific Taiwanese sausage are not reported in the HPB Foodo database as of 2026-04-16. 3. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB source for this record. 4. GI of 28 is an analogous estimate based on the sausage category in the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for Taiwanese sausage was found in any approved source. 5. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03301776 (pork sausage food product) and FoodEx2 A0FJP (Pork sausages) are used as closest defensible approximate matches; no Taiwan-specific sausage ontology code exists. 6. This food is not Singaporean in origin; therefore the Singaporean multilingual requirement (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) does not apply. Chinese-script names are provided as the food originates from Taiwan and is consumed in Chinese-speaking communities. Malay, Tamil, and Hindi names are not applicable for this Taiwanese food item.
